title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Evolution of Urban Architecture
content.subtitle: How 'biophilic design' is changing the way we build and live in cities.
content.text: For much of the 20th century, urban architecture was dominated by the 'Le Corbusier' ideal: the city as a 'machine for living.' This philosophy prioritized efficiency, concrete, and steel, often relegating nature to small, manicured parks that felt like an afterthought. However, as the psychological and environmental costs of 'gray' cities have become undeniable, a new movement has emerged: biophilic design. [31] The writer suggests that this is not just about adding plants to buildings, but about a fundamental integration of natural light, air, and organic shapes into the structural fabric of our homes and offices. [/31] The goal is to satisfy the innate human need to connect with the natural world, even in the heart of a metropolis. || The benefits of this approach are more than just aesthetic. Research has consistently shown that exposure to natural elements can reduce cortisol levels, improve cognitive function, and even speed up recovery times for hospital patients. [32] In a corporate context, biophilic offices have been linked to higher levels of employee creativity and a significant reduction in absenteeism. [/32] By moving away from the sterile, windowless cubicles of the past, companies are realizing that a healthier environment is also a more productive one. It is a shift from seeing buildings as static shelters to seeing them as 'ecosystems' that support human life. || [33] The author's tone when discussing the challenges of 'retrofitting' existing cities is one of pragmatic realism. [/33] While it is easy to design a new 'green' skyscraper from scratch, the real test lies in transforming the millions of aging, energy-inefficient structures that already exist. This requires innovative solutions, such as 'vertical forests'—balconies draped in thousands of trees and shrubs—and the 'daylighting' of buried rivers. These projects are expensive and technically complex, often facing resistance from traditional planners who prioritize immediate cost over long-term well-being. || Another point of intersection is the relationship between architecture and biodiversity. [34] Biophilic design seeks to create 'corridors' that allow wildlife to move through the urban landscape, turning buildings into habitats for birds, bees, and butterflies. [/34] This helps to combat the 'urban heat island' effect and improves local air quality. Instead of fighting against the environment, modern architects are learning to work with it, creating structures that breathe and evolve over time. It is a move toward 'regenerative' architecture, which aims to leave the environment better than it found it. || [35] However, some critics warn that biophilic design can be co-opted as 'greenwashing' for luxury real estate developments. [/35] There is a risk that access to 'nature-filled' living spaces will become yet another marker of wealth, leaving lower-income communities trapped in the same heat-trapping, concrete environments that were built decades ago. True biophilic design must be inclusive, focusing on public spaces, affordable housing, and transportation hubs to ensure that the benefits of nature are available to everyone, regardless of their social class. || Ultimately, the future of our cities depends on our ability to bridge the gap between the built and natural worlds. [36] The writer concludes that we must move beyond the view of nature as a 'decoration' and see it as a vital infrastructure for human health. [/36] As our global population continues to urbanize, the 'machine for living' must be replaced by the 'living machine.' By weaving nature back into the city, we are not just saving the environment; we are saving ourselves from the crushing anonymity of the concrete jungle. The city of the future should not just be a place where we work, but a place where we can truly thrive.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, what is the main goal of biophilic design according to the writer?
options: A) To make buildings look more like machines for living.
B) To create more manicured parks in the center of cities.
C) To integrate natural elements into the structure of the buildings themselves.
D) To replace all concrete and steel with wood and organic materials.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [31] and the mention of 'fundamental integration' of light, air, and shapes. A is the old model. B is what the author calls an 'afterthought.' D is too extreme.

questions.question: The second paragraph implies that biophilic offices...
options: A) are more expensive to maintain than traditional offices.
B) can lead to improved performance and health for workers.
C) are primarily designed to impress potential clients.
D) are only effective if they are located near a forest.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [32] and the mention of 'higher levels of employee creativity' and 'reduction in absenteeism.' A, C, and D are not mentioned.

questions.question: What is the author's view on transforming existing cities in the third paragraph?
options: A) He believes it is a simple process that only requires more funding.
B) He thinks it is a waste of time and that new cities should be built instead.
C) He recognizes that it is a difficult but necessary technical challenge.
D) He is optimistic that traditional planners will lead the way in this shift.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [33] and his tone of 'pragmatic realism' regarding 'retrofitting.' A is too simple. B is not suggested. D is contradicted by the mention of 'resistance' from traditional planners.

questions.question: According to the fourth paragraph, 'regenerative' architecture aims to...
options: A) protect buildings from being damaged by local wildlife.
B) reduce the number of birds and bees in urban areas.
C) have a positive impact on the surrounding environment.
D) make buildings as airtight as possible to improve air quality.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [34] and the goal of leaving the environment 'better than it found it.' A and B are the opposite of the author's point. D is contradicted by the mention of structures that 'breathe'.

questions.question: What concern do critics raise about biophilic design in the fifth paragraph?
options: A) That it will be too inclusive and reduce the value of luxury real estate.
B) That it is often used by developers to appear environmentally friendly without being so.
C) That it is not as effective as concrete at reducing the 'heat island' effect.
D) That it will eventually be replaced by a new, even more expensive trend.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [35] and the mention of 'greenwashing.' A is the opposite of the concern. C and D are not mentioned.

questions.question: Which of the following best summarizes the writer's conclusion?
options: A) Nature should be treated as an essential part of a city's foundation.
B) We should stop building cities and return to a more rural lifestyle.
C) The 'machine for living' is still the best model for modern architecture.
D) Decorating buildings with plants is enough to save the environment.
questions.explanation: A is supported by [36] and the call to see nature as 'vital infrastructure.' B is not suggested. C and D are the views the author argues against throughout the article.
